notes.anchor: first, Messala acquitted; then the same man condemned
notes.note: M. Valerius Messalla Rufus (cos. 53) was acquitted on the general bribery charge but then convicted under the stricter Lex Licinia de sodaliciis; Roman bribery prosecutions could be pressed twice under different statutes.

notes.anchor: P. Dolabella made one of the Fifteen
notes.note: The quindecimviri sacris faciundis, the priestly college of fifteen that guarded and consulted the Sibylline Books; co-option into it was a prestigious lifelong honour, here won by the young Dolabella.

notes.anchor: he had gone down to the Campus
notes.note: Elections were held on the Campus Martius outside the city; 'to go down to the Campus' is the idiom for standing as a candidate. Lentulus Crus had been confident of winning the priestly vacancy and lost.

notes.anchor: Servaeus, tribune-of-the-plebs designate, condemned, and C. Curio standing for the place in his stead
notes.note: Servaeus, elected tribune for 50 BC, was convicted before taking office, leaving the tribunate open; the unpredictable C. Scribonius Curio won the by-election, a turn that would prove decisive on the road to civil war.

notes.anchor: the proposal touching the dictatorship, the one we made fun of long ago
notes.note: M. Caelius Vinicianus, as tribune in 53 BC, had floated giving Pompey a dictatorship; the revival of that unpopular old scheme wrecked his candidacy at the aedilician elections.

notes.anchor: a session of the Senate had been held at the temple of Apollo
notes.note: The temple of Apollo Sosianus stood outside the sacred boundary (pomerium); the Senate met there so that Pompey, who held imperium and could not enter the city without forfeiting it, could attend in person.

notes.anchor: that legion which Pompey had set down to Caesar's account
notes.note: A legion Pompey had lent Caesar for the Gallic war; the squabble over recalling it, charged against Caesar's books, was an early skirmish in the dispute that led to civil war.
